text: A final episode with a slow pace (compared to previous ones), but very good vibes and fun. It's interesting to see how curiosity and goals drive human beings. Akane could have simply given up her interest in rakugo after what happened to her father, but she had been dreaming about it since childhood, and much of her achievements came from her own strength, although it's undeniable that she had the support of incredible people along the way. A lovely show, interesting characters, and well-paced development; may we receive a continuation as good as the first cour.

text: A stage name is typically bestowed on you soon after, if not right after, your master accepts you as a sign of you being formally under their care. It can, but doesn't have to, involve bits of your legal name (Yanagiya Kosan V famously gave most of his zenza the combination of 小 + an element of their legal name) Technically Akane isn't going by her legal name, as her stage name is in hiragana as contrasted to the kanji in her name. This sorta aligns with how many zenza tend to be given names at least partially in the (relatively) childish hiragana to emphasize their status as sort of children within their school, still having room for growth. And of course, there is the teigo (clan/school name) of Arakawa. (And while we're at it, PSA: do **NOT** refer to hanashika purely by their teigo.) I currently know of only one hanashika actually going by their legal given name and that's Hayashiya Sakutaro (and the fact he decided to do so was widely seen as quite unusual,) who is currently a futatsume.
label: analysis

text: **Second time at the cafe** As usual in this episode we get a very clever blend of SoL and action by having our ‘mission’ be a tour around Tokyo. We get a decent amount of time to roam around and let Chisataki have fun, before inevitably something goes wrong and we have some more stellar gunfight action between Jin and our team. Once again I commend the pacing, clever use of our entire cast of characters (shoutout to Mizuki for trying her best), decent twists and suspenseful moments to keep you guessing throughout the entire sequence. I actually forgot that the reveal of Chisato’s artificial heart was done so early, so I guess even as a rewatcher I was taken by surprise at Chisato randomly dropping it. I imagine the response of most first timers will mirror Takina’s “wait what?” The question gets partially answered throughout the episode with Mika explaining it to Kurumi and Chisato/Takina’s conversation on the boat. However, we are now left with further questions about its link to the Allen institute and Chisato’s ‘mission’. Very effective setup and payoffs that make me remember how I managed to binge this series so fast during my first watch. There is a bit more exploration of how the societal hierarchy works - Abe as a police detective is aware of the covering up happening, but chooses to be complacent and not prod into it, thinking that the ‘ends justifies the means’. Meanwhile this explanation takes the other detective by surprise and his first reaction is horror and bet

text: **Rewatcher** So Shinji kidnapped a dying patient from a hospital and hired an assassin to be killed by Chisato. Surely he has a good reason to do so, surely... Funnily, or tragically, that is what made her want to not take any more lives. And if the idea behind giving her that heart was making her execute her talent of killing people, there is some heavy conflict coming her way. Loved the action sequence this episode as well. In my memory, Takina did worse here and I didn't like that she had to be rescued, but she was doing fine. She only took a strafing hit and might have handled the situation on her own, but it was still good Chisato came to her aid. Unleashing the rubber bullets at point blank into Jin was a very cathartic finish. And even Mizuki had a chance to shine here. Also Kurumi has by now been fully adopted into the crew, which is nice. I also loved the little bit of panic in Takina's voice when she first heard about Chisato's artificial heart. She genuinely cares about Chisato now and is concerned for her, and Shion Wakayama's voice acting really shows off the layers in Takina's character. The little revelation that the police officer knew something about incidents getting covered up is great. As powerful as DA is, they cannot hide everything, particularly not from the police. Finally, Majim

Political apathy is the name of the game, and again, while tempting, this solely relies on the ones in charge actually prioritizing stability and good governance over power struggles. And with the ending of Majima applying pressure to DA, maybe they will crack and become draconian thus justifying him. We'll see. Now for the dialogue, Takina's concern looks so genuine when Chisato did not tell her about her heart. Takina is quite attentive, in every word of that definition if we consider the ending. label: analysis

text: Rewatcher. You know, I don't think I'd fully appreciated just how many moving parts this show has, because of how well it manages them all. DA's operations, Shinji and the Alan institute, Majima and his plans, Chisato's heart, Chisato's past, Mika's past (with Shinji), Takina's character arc, and Chisato and Takina's relationship--every single one of these pieces just gets one more little nugget of development at a time in a way that's really well-handled. I say that because I remember not initially thinking quite as much of this episode because the development of Takina and Chisato's relationship takes more of a backseat, but what we got instead is doing a very efficient job of moving the plot forward. I don't think I have much to say that others aren't going to be echoing. Learning about Chisato's heart, showing that Shinji and the Alan institute are no longer going to be passive about Chisato delivering her gift to the world, and my lord so much thematic foreshadowing. Kurumi shows quite a bit more of her sociopathic side this episode. Laughing at the TV broadcast, smiling at the idea of Mizuki having sacrificed herself. What a little weirdo. "I worked with Silent Jin for years, but I don't think I ever heard his voice." [Immediately upon capture] "Oh hey Mika, how's it going?" text: Hey, guys. Holofan4life here. Welcome to the Familiar of Zero 20th anniversary rewatch. Tsundere fans rejoice! This is the second part of a three part series involving rewatches of shows with Rie Kugimiya as the main female heroine. The first part was Shakugan no Shana, and the last will be Hayate the Combat Butler with a rewatch in 2027. For those who remember, I did a rewatch of Familiar of Zero in 2023. That was really when I started to dip my toe in the water in terms of doing rewatches for a full time basis. And I remember the show better better than its reputation suggests. I consider Familiar of Zero to be my guilty pleasure anime. It's not perfect, but it does a lot of stuff good. Also, it features a good mix of action, romance, comedy, drama, and Rie Kugimiya at arguably her most helplessly adorable. What's not to like? We'll get to it... I'm entirely doing this rewatch because I love tsunderes. And as extreme as the show can be at times, I have a massive soft spot for Louise and Saito and the world this show embodies. With that out of the way, let’s begin. I'm watching the sub, by the way. The back of my throat is so scratchy. I hope I'm not sick. If I don't get a job by tomorrow, I'mma lose it. Louise in a grassy field She sees Tabitha and Saito being close to each other. But Louise can't use her explosion magic. I can hear Rie Kugimiya say the word Explosion in English all day long. Can't use any magic And then Louise wakes up Well, that explains that weird screen
label: hot_take

text: Like I like the first 2/3rds of this season despite it apparently being weaker than the last 3rd ( Which, obviously, I disagree.). My issue is purely with the last 3rd on top of foundational issues the show's always had (I've been complaining about Louise not growing as a character. This isn't new.). I don't think dragging out a plot point that was ultimately meaningless (The familiar pact being broken.) and cutting out the fun stuff (the comedy) would fix the season and in fact feel it would make it worse. Like. Louise standing up to Henrietta was never going to work because the show hasn't put in the work to make it work. She's always happily offered to sacrifice herself for Henrietta or to do whatever Henrietta asks of her. There's been no hints that she might eventually snap. And frankly with how much stake she puts in her aristocrat title and her feelings on honor throwing that away for Tabitha feels out of character. Like I could maybe see her do it for Saito and even then that's questionable. And then there's Kirche. Kirche has barely been around after the first season. When would she have been given reason to start relying on Louise? Like unless they seeded the idea throughout the show prior it was never going to feel earned. text: Sadly it's never the "I love you"s or kisses that you remember. You remember that time you fought, all the times you've been at each others throats, that one time he leaned in to kiss someone else right in front of you (which is still his worst offence BY FAR). And you start to spiral and wonder if everything he said was a lie and if you're worthless like you always thought. And yet you can't bring yourself to break up because if you do you have absolutely nothing left What Holo did for you then is what Guitar-Bocchi did for me. Leaving your comfort zone and putting yourself out there is one of the bravest things you can do and I commend everyone that does it because I'm terrified of it. When BtR came out with this socially anxious mess of a main character that can barely hold a conversation, I finally felt seen in a way I never had before. And it got so absurdly popular out of nowhere that a lot of people must've felt the same way. I try to put myself out there at least a little more these days and BtR is no small part of the reason for that, but sometimes I still just don't wanna talk to anyone for a week
label: analysis

text: **Part 2** Overall, I thought this was a satisfying way to end the season. It was like Louise's crowning achievement as a character after so many L's taken and her emotions getting the better of her. The action was great and easily the most exciting since the Menville arc, and Saito and Louise had a couple heartfelt moments that gave the episode weight. I'd like to think this will officially mark a change in their relationship, but with nothing official it's hard to say. I do think it was clever to have the episode be about Louise worrying about losing her Void mage powers. That made for a good parallel to the beginning of the season which was about Saito losing his Gandalfr powers. A lot of interesting directions were set up in this episode. Louise is now officially someone of importance, and Joseph and Sheffield are still around causing chaos. It feels like they will be the main antagonists heading into the series finale. I could see a situation where with Louise and Tabitha now in prominent positions of power, the war from season 2 gets resumed and Tristain takes a more assertive approach. To let Joseph carry on when he's committed crimes against humanity seems a bit like turning a blind eye. It also feels like the end of Tabitha's character arc, and I thought that was handled well. It was one of the better parts of season 3 and helped prevent the season from being a total waste. I wonder if we'll see Tabitha bond with her mother now that she's awake and seems to have gain
label: analysis

text: They were only able to massacre the village in that fashion because Asa broke the seal with break; Asa is just as complicit in the murders of everyone in the village as Gabby is. Why does she get a pass? I think the narrative treating Gabby (and most of the Kagemori's) with agency outside of just being one-dimensional killers is a good thing contrary to the this kind of thinking I often see. The narrative is treating the Kagemori's in a neutral light instead of guiding the viewer on how to feel about them. I feel like this is just "trust in the viewer" and not anything sinister like excusing their actions and people aren't used to this level of neutrality with these topics. The series does not whitewash the fact that they're lowbrow Yakuza, yet the fact remains that they all think they're right/justified in their own way so of course we see scenes of them chilling, hanging out, and acting in a sympathetic light. To them, they're the protagonists of their story. They're justified and their crimes don't weigh on them, that is how they (and actual "murderers with a code") continue to smile day to day in real life. Despite their crimes they're all humans with their own justification and not caricatures of whatever you think murderers should be. You're supposed to come to your own feelings on them by judging their actions day to day instead of the author automatically condemning them and going "these guys are all evil". Am I the only one who finds it to be mature writing? We don't
label: analysis

text: I admit I haven't liked it quite as much as Hiromu Arakawa's other works, but she's my favorite mangaka so that's a high bar. I definitely like a lot of the characters - I especially agree with really liking the relationship between Yuru and Right and Left. And I also really like Yuru himself - he's a typical shonen protagonist in some ways but unique in other ways, so he really works well in his role. He's relatable and endearing but also surprisingly cool and capable.
label: analysis

text: Sharp and tight is exactly how I'd describe the show, too. It hasn't (yet) reached that "excellent" status for me by any means, as there's something missing in it that I can't put my finger on, but I can't help but really, really like how the characters are written and how the narrative feels multi-dimensional. And sure, it's often a lot of just talking and talking which causes the show to suffer since it's a visual medium (this isn't me saying there should be more battles, by the way, just wishing they utilized more "showing" than "tell") but the writing is just that good that I get sucked into it and the episode passes by before I realize. Everyone's got hidden motives and there's something new to learn about a character or the greater narrative every episode. I'm just waiting for the ball to drop at this point and see the world lore or daemon stuff to open things up beyond this rival clan narrative. It'll probably happen when Yuru finally gets Seal.
label: analysis

text: I just think that's a rather cynical way of looking at it, especially coming from an author who wrote a similar dynamic for the war arc of their other popular work that needn't be named. The story doesn't go "actually the village was bad, so it's fine." It shows why the Kagemori think the village is bad, and how they justify their indiscriminate murder of the people there. This is another case of viewers thinking what the characters are thinking is endorsement from the authors side rather than just what is being presented, and it's kind of frustrating. I feel like that's where "trust in the viewer" comes in. Obviously no one is going to turn to the camera and say "that was fucked up Gabby" because she's surrounded by people who endorsed and coordinated the attack. Yuru has expressed his anger with Gabby's actions already, and that's about all we'll get. Instead we get scenes like in today's episode where we get a follow-up on the kid who she traumatized for life by murdering her mother in front of her so we as the viewers can make up our own minds on her character and actions. We see the result of her killings affecting people who weren't even apart of the conflict she's taking part in. This is trust in the viewer, this is subtlety in writing that doesn't want to tell you how to think. I just think anime is so unused to this kind of writing that people don't know how to grapple with it sometimes.
label: analysis

text: Agreed, it's sitting at a solid 7 right now with the (high) potential to go up. I suppose that 'something missing' for me is probably a clearer goal? Right now we hardly know what everyone wants to achieve, and the MC only has the vaguely defined goal of finding his parents with no concrete plan on how to do so. So it feels like we're getting worldbuilding and lore stuff for the sake of it. Good worldbuilding mind you, but still feels a tad bit meandering. Agreed on the more showing stuff, but to the show's credit it at least tries to imply more than what the characters talked about, which is more than can be said for most shonen(?) shows. E.g. the villagers in the last episode casually talking about Seal, implying they had the motivation and probably a hand in keep the MC oblivious and trapped in the village.
label: hot_take

text: Right now it feels very ~*Okay*~. It's not bad per se but i'm not exactly at the edge of my seat excited for next week's episode. I have high expectations having really enjoyed both FMA and Silver Spoon so i don't mind riding out a slow burn start.

text: I'm also not a huge fan of how the main character is being written. He has little to no agency in this story, and I am not emotionally engaged with his journey to find his parents at all. I know others feel he is unique, but I find his character rather bland and for some reason he is always participating in the fights. The fact that daemons don't immediately outclass him, specifically the stunt in the latest episode, pulls me out unfortunately.
label: hot_take

text: Portraying Asa and Gabby as these cute, quirky girls despite committing a massacre was really the worst choice the mangaka made. Asa having a brother complex is also nauseating, like I can understand Asa having those feelings but the show goes over the top in portraying it, the only scene that I felt was tone appropriate was when Asa hugged Yuru as he left the Kagemori estate. If only the mangaka stopped portraying Asa's bro-con as a gag or comedy and more as this bittersweet longing, then maybe Asa would be more palatable. The tonal whiplash is my main problem with this show.
label: analysis

text: Yeah, \[spoilers for the first few episodes\] at some point it becomes clear that episode 1 was done purely for shock value, which is so bad because it makes the subsequent writing feel really dumb. The protagonist's entire village is massacred in a sadistic way, then before anyone comes to terms with that, the perpetrators start to get portrayed as all cute and cuddly, with Yuru joining them and barely asking any questions about the massacre. That for me was enough to drop the series. I'm sure later on there was going to be a reveal that actually the villagers were the true bad guys or whatever, but if that comes to pass, it's a very silly and predictable setup, but more importantly, it doesn't explain how Yuru was so chill around these people. I also *heavily* dislike the idea of an entire village being portrayed as collectively guilty of a crime worthy of mass execution (if that's where the story was going.) That's the kind of thing that makes me write-off someone as an author and start to question their motivations. In hindsight I think if they did episode 1 differently and didn't go hard on the edgelord shock value I probably could have stayed with this show a bit more just to see how they were going to justify things. But even then the chances of it being convincing was still gonna be very low.
label: analysis

text: The Wiseman's grandchild should be the baseline for generic isekai. It explains multiple reasons why MC is overpowered like that hes raised by the 2 most powerful magic users and how his magic is boosted by his scientific understanding of the spells and how they affect the world. There is no harem at all, the guy gets one girl interested in him and a bunch of classmates of both genders that he teaches to also be overpowered. And the fan service is minimal. During the obligatory hotspring episode, the character that gets a pan of their naked body (obscured by water) is the grandma. The show is by no means amazing, but it does it's job well and doesn't really make any mistakes or offend anyone. Its a solid baseline for a 6/10. Better than average, but not by much
label: analysis
